revisions: CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'Anwar called Norway's cancellation contracts not confetti and Malaysia is weighing a lawsuit' → 'weighing legal action' (Bernama/Reuters: assessing claims, not filed lawsuit)

CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'Malaysia had paid ~RM550m' → 'Malaysia had paid nearly 95% of the €124m contract — more than RM500 million' (using Khaled Nordin's actual phrasing per The Star 14 May 2026; RM550m was a derived estimate)

CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'The first ship those missiles were meant for hasn't been delivered yet' → 'had begun sea trials but had not been delivered to the navy' (Bernama 28 Jan 2026 confirms LCS-1 began initial sea trials in January; full sea trials in late April per LUNAS/Naval News)

CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'Zero convictions across 15 years of investigation' → 'Zero convictions across the 15-year programme history' (Stage 2 and Stage 3 both flagged: formal MACC/PAC/JKSTUPKK probes only commenced post-2018, so ~7 years of investigation, not 15)

CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'advance payment to BNS ran at 136× the Treasury threshold' → 'BNS received advance payments of up to RM1.36 billion against a Treasury Circular threshold of RM10m' (precise PAC formulation; the 136× is the derived multiple, not the citation)

CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'RM400m of LCS funds was diverted to retire a prior naval programme's debt' → 'used to settle old NGPV-programme debts' (Malay Mail 4 Aug 2022 PAC chairman's actual phrasing — 'diverted' is harsher than the source)
CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'The only criminal charge' → 'The only publicly reported LCS criminal prosecution' (Stage 3: full AGC/MACC prosecution register not separately checked)
CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'RM11.145bn ceiling per PAC' → 'per the declassified JKSTUPKK report' (PAC media statement and JKSTUPKK both used; the RM11.145bn figure is from the declassified JKSTUPKK report)

CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'Norway invoked force majeure — an enumerated contract clause, not a breach. The contract worked exactly as it should' → 'Norway invoked force majeure. Norwegian regulations require arms contracts to be subject to licence approval (§22) and explicitly allow revocation when circumstances change (§25). The Malaysia–Kongsberg contract text isn't public, so a clean no-breach conclusion can't be drawn; Malaysia is assessing breach-of-contract claims' (USNI News confirms force majeure invocation by MINDEF Sec-Gen Lokman Hakim Ali; the broader 'no breach' legal conclusion was unverified)

CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'There is no public precedent of an importer state winning a suit against an exporter state' → 'No directly comparable importer-state-wins precedent was identified in this review' (Stage 3: a negative global case-law claim requires comprehensive legal research not done)

CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'sovereign immunity, act-of-state doctrine and standard force-majeure clauses combine against such suits' → 'Any suit would face state immunity, governing-law limits, and the contract's force-majeure wording' (reframed as risk factors rather than legal conclusion; jurisdiction-specific doctrines)

CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'On the same day Malaysia was cut off, Australia signed a Strike Missile Family MoU' → 'On the day Malaysia's dispute became public, Australia signed a multilateral Strike Missile Family MoU with Norway and 10 other NSM/JSM users' (revocation actually March 2026; MoU 14 May 2026; Stage 5 also flagged this as Card 4's weakness — both stages agreed)

CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'Australia, which recognised Palestine in November 2025' → 'Australia, which formally recognised Palestine on 21 September 2025' (Stage 2 and Stage 3 both flagged this; Australian PM joint statement 21 Sept 2025)

CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'Malaysia was the only non-NATO and non-NATO-partner customer in Kongsberg's NSM book — every other operator is NATO or partner' → 'every other selected operator is NATO or NATO partner' (Stage 3: Malaysia had selected NSM but had not received or operated it; distinguish selected customer from operator)

CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'The discriminator was alliance classification' → 'Norway's stated criterion was allies and closest partners — a partner-access framing, which Malaysia was not' (Stage 3: 'discriminator' is an OVERCLAIM since no formal country-tier decision document has been published)

CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'using Oslo to bury 15 years of unaccounted billions on ships the missiles were never going to fit on' → 'on ships still undelivered when the missiles had already been paid for' (Stage 3 INCORRECT: Kongsberg's own 2018 press release states NSM would be deck-mounted and integrated to Naval Group's SETIS — directly contradicts 'never going to fit')

CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'eight months before any ship existed to mount them on' → 'nine months before the first ship's scheduled handover; LCS-1 had begun sea trials but had not been delivered to the navy' (Stage 3: first ship physically existed and was in sea trials; March-to-December is approximately nine months)

CORRECTED (Stage 3): 'Norway exercised a clause Malaysia agreed to' → 'Norway invoked force majeure under its own export-control regime' (Stage 3: force-majeure invocation verified; 'Malaysia agreed to the exact clause' is unverified since contract text not public)
ADDED (Stage 3 omitted_facts): Programme has been reduced from six to five ships; first delivery now December 2026 — incorporated into Fact 1's tail
ADDED (Stage 3 omitted_facts): Norwegian Eksportforskriften §22 (proviso requirement) and §25 (revocation circumstances) — incorporated into Fact 2 to anchor the legal mechanism
ADDED (Stage 4 missing_facts): Government Procurement Act 2025 still lacks defence-specific provisions (C4 Center via FMT 8 Jan 2026) — incorporated into the View card

ADDED (Stage 4 international_parallels): India's CBI zero convictions across major defence procurement cases over four decades (The Wire); Indonesia AW101 case only convicted the middleman, not senior officials (Declassified UK) — incorporated into the View card to show the pattern is structural in regional defence procurement

STRENGTHENED (Stage 5): Reframe and View now name the cross-administration impunity pattern directly — 'one DNAA on dementia grounds remains the only criminal endpoint across four governments' (PAC 2022 + Sessions Court DNAA March 2025, both primary-source-backed; Stage 5: this is verifiable, not partisan)

STRENGTHENED (Stage 5): View card folds in the structural-extraction reading — political and contractor class faces negligible downside while public coffers fund undelivered assets — without losing primary-source discipline

INTRODUCED (Stage 6 self-verified): 'Multilateral Strike Missile Family MoU with Norway and 10 other NSM/JSM users' — verified against Australian Defence Ministers media release 14 May 2026 and Stage 4 confirmation of multilateral structure

INTRODUCED (Stage 6 self-verified): 'Both Norway (May 2024) and Australia (September 2025) formally recognised Palestine, undermining any Palestine-retaliation theory for the revocation' — verified against regjeringen.no 22 May 2024 statement and Australian PM joint statement 21 September 2025

cards.text: Anwar called Norway's cancellation 'contracts not confetti' and Malaysia is weighing legal action. The first ship those missiles were meant for had begun sea trials but had not been delivered to the navy.
cards.sub: Norway revoked the Naval Strike Missile export approvals in March 2026. LCS-1 KD Maharaja Lela began initial sea trials in January 2026 and is scheduled for handover in December. Malaysia had paid nearly 95% of the €124m contract — more than RM500 million.

cards.h: Zero LCS hulls delivered. RM6.083 billion paid to Boustead Naval Shipyard. Zero convictions across the 15-year programme history — formal investigations only began after 2018.
cards.s: The 2022 PAC media statement and the declassified JKSTUPKK forensic report found the programme ceiling rose from RM9b to RM11.145b, BNS received advance payments of up to RM1.36 billion against a Treasury Circular threshold of RM10m, and RM400m of LCS funds was used to settle old NGPV-programme debts. The only publicly reported LCS criminal prosecution — RM21.08m CBT against ex-BNS MD Tan Sri Ahmad Ramli — ended in a dementia-grounded discharge in March 2025. The programme has since been reduced from six to five ships, with first delivery scheduled for December 2026.
cards.d: Parlimen.gov.my PAC media statement 4 Aug 2022; JKSTUPKK declassified report 17 Aug 2022; The Star 17 Aug 2022 and 13 Mar 2025; Malay Mail 4 Aug 2022 and 12 Mar 2025

cards.h: Norway invoked force majeure. Norwegian export regulations require arms contracts to be subject to licence approval and explicitly allow revocation when circumstances change.
cards.s: MINDEF Sec-Gen Lokman Hakim Ali confirmed Norway invoked force majeure. Norwegian Eksportforskriften §22 requires the licence-approval proviso in arms-export agreements; §25 allows revocation. The Malaysia–Kongsberg contract text isn't public, so a clean no-breach conclusion can't be drawn — Malaysia is expressly assessing breach-of-contract claims. Any Malaysian suit would face state immunity, governing-law limits, and the contract's force-majeure wording. No directly comparable importer-state-wins precedent was identified in this review.
cards.d: USNI News 14 May 2026 (Lokman Hakim Ali statement); Norwegian Eksportforskriften consolidated 19 Sept 2025 (regjeringen.no §§22, 25); SIPRI 2025 commentary on politicisation of export controls

cards.h: On the day Malaysia's dispute became public, Australia signed a multilateral Strike Missile Family MoU with Norway and 10 other NSM/JSM users.
cards.s: Norway had pulled Malaysia's export approvals in March 2026; the dispute went public at prime-ministerial level on 14 May 2026, the same day Australia signed the multilateral MoU. Malaysia was the only non-NATO and non-NATO-partner selected customer in Kongsberg's NSM book; every other selected operator is NATO or NATO partner. Both Norway (May 2024) and Australia (21 September 2025) formally recognised Palestine, undermining any Palestine-retaliation theory. Norway's stated criterion was 'allies and closest partners' — a partner-access framing, which Malaysia was not.
cards.d: Australian Defence Ministers media release 14 May 2026; Reuters 14 May 2026; Kongsberg 2026 NSM/JSM brochure; regjeringen.no Palestine recognition 22 May 2024; Australian PM joint statement 21 Sept 2025

cards.h: The scandal is not Norway revoking an export licence. The scandal is using Oslo to bury 15 years of LCS billions on ships still undelivered, while one DNAA on dementia grounds remains the only criminal endpoint across four governments.
Like paying 95% upfront for a fitting tailored to a suit not yet stitched — then suing the tailor when his country bans the fabric.
cards.text: LCS-1 had begun sea trials but had not been delivered to the navy when the revocation arrived. The first ship's scheduled handover is December 2026 — about nine months after Norway pulled the approvals.

Norway invoked force majeure under its own export-control regime. The deeper cost is 15 years of LCS programme history that produced no delivered ships, one DNAA on dementia grounds as the only criminal endpoint, and no senior accountability across four governments. India's CBI has secured zero convictions in major defence procurement cases over four decades; Indonesia's AW101 prosecution reached only the middleman. The pattern is structural, not Malaysian-exceptional. The Government Procurement Act 2025 still lacks defence-specific provisions, so the next round of the same problem is already engineered. Future contracts must price licence-revocation risk; unfinished LCS accountability cases should not vanish in a foreign-policy news cycle.

introduced_claims.claim: Multilateral Strike Missile Family MoU with Norway and 10 other NSM/JSM users (i.e., 12 countries total)
introduced_claims.primary_source: Australian Defence Ministers media release 14 May 2026 (https://www.minister.defence.gov.au/media-releases/2026-05-14/albanese-government-signs-defence-mou-norway-helping-make-australia-more-self-reliant-boosting-regional-security); cross-confirmed via Stage 4 reporting

introduced_claims.claim: Both Norway (May 2024) and Australia (21 September 2025) formally recognised Palestine, undermining any Palestine-retaliation theory
introduced_claims.primary_source: regjeringen.no 'Norway recognises Palestine as a state' 22 May 2024; Australian PM joint statement 'Australia recognises State of Palestine' 21 Sept 2025

introduced_claims.claim: 'About nine months after Norway pulled the approvals' (March 2026 → December 2026 handover)
introduced_claims.primary_source: Derived arithmetic from verified dates: Reuters/AP confirm Norway pulled approvals in March 2026; Bernama 28 Jan 2026 and The Star 14 May 2026 confirm December 2026 handover

introduced_claims.claim: The pattern is structural, not Malaysian-exceptional (India CBI zero convictions over four decades; Indonesia AW101 only middleman convicted)
introduced_claims.primary_source: Stage 4 verified additions: The Wire March 2025 on India CBI; Declassified UK on Indonesia AW101

introduced_claims.claim: 'The next round of the same problem is already engineered' — rhetorical compression of the structural argument
introduced_claims.primary_source: Supported by Stage 4 fact: GPA 2025 lacks defence procurement provisions (C4 Center via FMT 8 Jan 2026). Rhetorical framing is editorial inference, but the underlying structural premise is primary-source backed.
introduced_claims.verified: framing is editorial; underlying fact verified

synthesis_notes: Three over-integration decisions to flag. (1) Stage 4 offered a counter-argument that the same-day Australia MoU does NOT fully disprove a Palestine-causation theory because 'Australia recognised Palestine in November 2025 and Norway recognised Palestine in May 2024 — both are Palestine-recognising states.' Stage 4's reasoning is itself logically inverted: if both the punishing state (Norway) and the rewarded state (Australia) are Palestine recognisers, a 'Norway punishes Malaysia for being pro-Palestine' theory is incoherent, not strengthened. We kept the Palestine disproof and explicitly added both recognition facts to Fact 3 to make the disproof concrete. (Note also that Stage 4 itself reported the wrong Australia date — November 2025 instead of the verified 21 September 2025 — the same error Stage 2 and Stage 3 independently flagged in the Stage 1 text.) (2) Stage 5 wanted more aggressive elite-impunity naming; Stage 2 found near-zero political bias in the original draft. We resolved by sharpening the Reframe and View cards with the cross-administration impunity language Stage 5 requested, using the same primary sources (PAC 2022, Sessions Court DNAA 2025) the Stage 2 audit had already cleared as non-partisan. This is not averaging — it is preserving Stage 5's punch where the evidence supports it. (3) Stage 4 raised five missing community perspectives (Bumiputera SMEs, East Malaysia, women in MAF, youth, Indian Malaysians) and the rebus sic stantibus counter-argument. The bite-size budget cannot carry all five plus the structural reframe; we incorporated the two most editorially load-bearing additions (the GPA 2025 gap and the India/Indonesia structural-impunity comparator) into the View card, and noted the remaining perspectives in source_notes for future related issues. Stage 2's RMN-design-switch omission flag is partially addressed in the brief (Zahid Hamidi reversed the Navy's Damen Sigma preference on 11 July 2011); the cards do not have room for that detail, but the brief carries it. Stage 2's recourse/refund evasion flag is now partly addressed in Fact 2 by surfacing the legal-risk factors rather than asserting 'no recovery possible' — Malaysia's recourse pathway is acknowledged as live but constrained. Compression check: the only qualifier knowingly compressed is 'roughly nine months' (Stage 3's exact wording) → 'about nine months' in the analogy card; both phrasings preserve the qualification. No claim has been compressed in a way that drops a load-bearing caveat.
source_notes: Stage 4 raised five missing community perspectives that the bite-size budget cannot carry in a single issue: Bumiputera SME and local-worker dependency (Ismail Sabri 2021: 1,600 jobs / 400 SMEs), East Malaysian security framing (LCS-3 KD Sharif Mashor naming, Sabah/Sarawak logistics gap), women in MAF (8% vs 10% Defence White Paper target), youth/Muda perspective on 'Ali Baba' procurement, and the Indian-Malaysian-community angle on Bumiputera procurement preferences. These should be carried into related future issues rather than crammed into this one. Stage 4 counter-argument on rebus sic stantibus is interesting but speculative and unsourced beyond own-reasoning; not asserted in cards. Stage 5 predictions (6m fade / 2y repeat) are appropriately labelled as HYPOTHESIS in Stage 5's own output; we did not import them into cards as facts. Norwegian-language primary press (Dagbladet, VG, Aftenposten, Forsvarets Forum, Dagsavisen) was indirectly accessed via search snippets; the Norwegian government statement we rely on is regjeringen.no English-language docs plus Reuters/NTB/AP wire pickup of Guri Solberg's English statement — adequate but not redundant. Stage 4's source for the Norwegian MFA's exact wording (Dagsavisen) corroborates the line we already had via Breaking Defense/Euronews, which is a useful cross-check.
